ip_outcome_note: Card name matches species name exactly. Depicted figure — swirling dark-energy vortex with green glow and ghostly face patterns, anchored to a cracked stone keystone — matches the canonical visual design of Spiritomb (National Pokedex no. 442). API confirms nationalPokedexNumbers: [442]. No ambiguity; Spiritomb verified.

trivia_bullets.text: Spiritomb (National Pokedex no. 442) is designed around a Buddhist and Chinese literary tradition. The number 108 references the 108 temptations one must overcome to achieve Nirvana in Buddhist tradition — Japanese Buddhist temples ring their bells 108 times on New Year's Eve for this reason. Spiritomb may also reference Water Margin, one of China's Four Great Classical Novels, in which a stone monument containing 108 sealed demons is disturbed by a foolish marshal, releasing them. The Odd Keystone design (a trapezoidal stone with two dots and a fissure resembling a frown) is the physical prison anchoring the spirits. [Bulbapedia: Spiritomb, via WebSearch]
"Exactly 108 spirits gathered to become this Pokemon. Apparently there are some ill-natured spirits in the mix." `[PokemonTCG.io API: swsh9-89 flavorText]`

Spiritomb may additionally be based on a Jibakurei (a spirit bound to a single place in Japanese folklore) or a Deildegast (a Norwegian folkloric spirit forced to haul a boundary stone as punishment for moving one while alive). The Odd Keystone-as-prison motif works across all three reference points: punishment, binding, and displacement from the natural world. [Bulbapedia: Spiritomb, via WebSearch]

The BRS Spiritomb (no. 089/172) is a common Darkness-type with two effects: Ticking Terror (shift the defending Pokemon's Weakness to Darkness until end of next turn) and Cursed Drop (place 2 damage counters on opponent's Pokemon in any distribution). The Weakness-reassignment effect is rare in the TCG and pairs well with Darkness-type attackers. Introduced in Generation IV (Diamond and Pearl, 2006), Spiritomb originally had no type weaknesses — the only Pokemon alongside Sableye with this distinction, until the Fairy type introduction in Generation VI gave both a weakness. [PokemonTCG.io API: swsh9-89]
